docs.title: drf-flex-fields2
docs.text: drf-flex-fields2 adds dynamic field expansion, sparse fieldsets, and nested serializer control to Django REST Framework serializers with a small API surface and minimal magic.

docs.title: What it gives you
docs.text:
• Expand nested resources on demand with query parameters such as ?expand=country.states.
  • Return sparse fieldsets with ?fields= or ?omit=.
  • Reuse serializers instead of maintaining multiple slim variants.
  • Keep list endpoints under control with per-view expansion limits.
  • Optionally optimize querysets with a dedicated filter backend.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• If you are new to the package, read Installation and Quick Start.
  • If you already know the basics, jump to Usage or Advanced Topics.
  • If you need the code surface, see API Reference.

docs.title: Supported stack
docs.text:
• Python 3.12 and 3.13
  • Django 6.x
  • Django REST Framework 3.17+

docs.title: Documentation versions
docs.text: The published documentation is intended to be hosted on Read the Docs with one version per Git tag. The repository contains the required .readthedocs.yaml and mkdocs.yml files; the remaining one-time project configuration is documented in Repository Setup.

docs.title: 2.0.0 (April 2026)
docs.text:
• Project forked from rsinger86/drf-flex-fields.
  • Python package renamed to drf-flex-fields2
  • Migrated tooling from setuptools to poetry.
  • Added CI tooling for automated version upgrades and SBOM generation.
  • Migrated source code from Python 2 to Python 3 syntax.
  • Fixed usage of deprecated Django APIs.
  • Improved type hints and static typing compatibility (e.g., for PyLance).

docs.title: 1.0.2 (March 2023)
docs.text: • Adds control over whether recursive expansions are allowed and allows setting the max expansion depth. Thanks @andruten!

docs.title: 1.0.1 (March 2023)
docs.text: • Various bug fixes. Thanks @michaelschem, @andruten, and @erielias!

docs.title: 1.0.0 (August 2022)
docs.text: • Improvements to the filter backends for generic foreign key handling and docs generation. Thanks @KrYpTeD974 and @michaelschem!

docs.title: 0.9.9 (July 2022)
docs.text:
• Fixes bug in FlexFieldsFilterBackend. Thanks @michaelschem!
  • Adds FlexFieldsDocsFilterBackend for schema population. Thanks @Rjevski!

docs.title: 0.9.8 (April 2022)
docs.text: • Set expandable fields as the default example for expand query parameters in coreapi.Field. Thanks @JasperSui!

docs.title: 0.9.7 (January 2022)
docs.text: • Includes m2m in prefetch_related clause even if they're not expanded. Thanks @pablolmedorado and @ADR-007!

docs.title: 0.9.6 (November 2021)
docs.text: • Make it possible to use wildcard values with sparse fields requests.

docs.title: 0.9.5 (October 2021)
docs.text:
• Adds OpenAPI support. Thanks @soroush-tabesh!
  • Updates tests for Django 3.2 and fixes deprecation warning. Thanks @giovannicimolin!

docs.title: 0.9.3 (August 2021)
docs.text: • Fixes bug where custom parameter names were not passed when constructing nested serializers. Thanks @Kandeel4411!

docs.title: 0.9.2 (June 2021)
docs.text: • Ensures context dict is passed down to expanded serializers. Thanks @nikeshyad!

docs.title: 0.9.1 (June 2021)
docs.text: • No longer auto removes source argument if it's equal to the field name.

docs.title: 0.9.0 (April 2021)
docs.text: • Allows fully qualified import strings for lazy serializer classes.

docs.title: 0.8.9 (February 2021)
docs.text: • Adds OpenAPI support to experimental filter backend. Thanks @LukasBerka!

docs.title: 0.8.8 (September 2020)
docs.text:
• Django 3.1.1 fix. Thansks @NiyazNz!
  • Docs typo fix. Thanks @zakjholt!

docs.title: 0.8.6 (September 2020)
docs.text: • Adds is_included utility function.

docs.title: 0.8.5 (May 2020)
docs.text: • Adds options to customize parameter names and wildcard values. Closes #10.

docs.title: 0.8.1 (May 2020)
docs.text: • Fixes #44, related to the experimental filter backend. Thanks @jsatt!

docs.title: 0.8.0 (April 2020)
docs.text:
• Adds support for expand, omit and fields query parameters for non-GET requests.
  • The common use case is creating/updating a model instance and returning a serialized response with expanded fields
  • Thanks @kotepillar for raising the issue (#25) and @Crocmagnon for the idea of delaying field modification to to_representation().

docs.title: 0.7.5 (February 2020)
docs.text:
• Simplifies declaration of expandable_fields
  • If using a tuple, the second element - to define the serializer settings - is now optional.
  • Instead of a tuple, you can now just use the serializer class or a string to lazily reference that class.
  • Updates documentation.

docs.title: 0.7.0 (February 2020)
docs.text:
• Adds support for different ways of passing arrays in query strings. Thanks @sentyaev!
  • Fixes attribute error when map is supplied to split levels utility function. Thanks @hemache!

docs.title: 0.6.1 (September 2019)
docs.text:
• Adds experimental support for automatically SQL query optimization via a FlexFieldsFilterBackend. Thanks ADR-007!
  • Adds CircleCI config file. Thanks mikeIFTS!
  • Moves declaration of expandable_fields to Meta class on serialzer for consistency with DRF (will continue to support declaration as class property)
  • Python 2 is no longer supported. If you need Python 2 support, you can continue to use older versions of this package.

docs.title: 0.5.0 (April 2019)
docs.text: • Added support for omit keyword for field exclusion. Code clean up and improved test coverage.

docs.title: 0.3.4 (May 2018)
docs.text: • Handle case where request is None when accessing request object from serializer. Thanks @jsatt!

docs.title: 0.3.3 (April 2018)
docs.text: • Exposes FlexFieldsSerializerMixin in addition to FlexFieldsModelSerializer. Thanks @jsatt!

docs.title: Repository and Tooling Setup
docs.text:
This document describes the repository configuration that keeps CI, dependency management, and published documentation working.

  1. GitHub Repository Settings
  2. Repository Secrets
  3. Branch Protection Rules
  4. Read the Docs Project Settings
  5. Root Configuration Files

docs.title: GitHub Repository Settings
docs.text: Some project behavior still depends on settings outside the repository.

docs.title: Repository Secrets
docs.text:
Under Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions, create the following repository secret:

Secret name Description RENOVATE_TOKEN Fine-grained personal access token for Renovate and SBOM automation.

RENOVATE_TOKEN is used to create pull requests and to merge auto-mergeable dependency updates after checks pass.

The token must belong to a user with write access to the repository and needs:

  • Read access to metadata
  • Read and write access to code, issues, pull requests, and workflows

docs.title: Branch Protection Rules
docs.text: Under Settings → Rules → Rulesets or Settings → Branches, apply the following rules to the default branch.

docs.title: Require a pull request before merging
docs.text:
Direct pushes to the default branch should be blocked. All changes should go through a pull request.

The required status check is tests, which is the final aggregator job from .github/workflows/run-tests.yml. That workflow routes changes either to the full test suite or to the dummy success workflow depending on whether relevant code changed.

This keeps branch protection strict for package changes while avoiding expensive test runs for unrelated repository maintenance.

docs.title: Automatically request a Copilot code review
docs.text: Under Settings → General → Pull Requests, enable Automatically request Copilot code review.

docs.title: Read the Docs Project Settings
docs.text:
The repository now includes .readthedocs.yaml and mkdocs.yml, but Read the Docs still requires one-time project configuration.

Recommended setup:

  • Import the GitHub repository into Read the Docs.
  • Keep main as the default branch.
  • Ensure the Read the Docs GitHub webhook stays enabled so pushes and tags are synced automatically.
  • Use the checked-in .readthedocs.yaml file instead of configuring build commands manually in the UI.
  • In Admin → Automation Rules or the current versions UI, add a rule that activates release tags automatically.
  • If releases are tagged as v2.0.0, use a pattern such as v*. Use * only if every Git tag should become a published docs version.
  • Keep latest as the default version and optionally promote the newest release to stable.

No additional GitHub secret is required for tag-triggered Read the Docs builds when the repository is connected through the native GitHub integration.

docs.title: pyproject.toml
docs.text:
The central project file managed by Poetry. It contains:

  • Project metadata such as package name, version, authors, and repository URL
  • Runtime dependency definitions
  • Documentation dependency definitions in [tool.poetry.group.docs.dependencies]

docs.title: poetry.lock
docs.text: Generated by Poetry and committed to the repository so every environment resolves identical dependency versions.

docs.title: renovate.json
docs.text: Configuration for Renovate. It manages Poetry and GitHub Actions dependencies, separates major from minor upgrades, and supports automated merges for safe updates.

docs.title: mkdocs.yml
docs.text: Primary documentation-site configuration. Defines navigation, Markdown extensions, the Material theme, and mkdocstrings integration for API reference pages.

docs.title: .readthedocs.yaml
docs.text: Read the Docs build configuration. It installs Poetry, installs the project together with the docs dependency group, and builds the site with MkDocs.

docs.title: doc/
docs.text: Source directory for the documentation site. It now contains the user guide, API reference pages, changelog, and maintainer notes.

docs.title: .github/workflows/build-docs.yml
docs.text: Builds the MkDocs site in CI on documentation, packaging, and public API changes. This keeps local docs changes aligned with what Read the Docs will build.

docs.title: .github/workflows/run-tests.yml
docs.text: Routes pull requests either to the full Django test suite or the dummy check, depending on whether relevant code changed.

docs.title: .editorconfig
docs.text: Editor-wide formatting defaults, including four-space indentation and LF line endings.

docs.title: .gitattributes
docs.text: Git attributes for generated or special-case files.

docs.title: .gitignore
docs.text: Ignore rules for Python cache directories, virtual environments, and local build artifacts.

docs.title: Customization
docs.text:
Parameter names and wildcard values can be configured with the Django setting REST_FLEX_FIELDS.

Option Description Default EXPAND_PARAM Query parameter name for requested expansions. "expand" MAXIMUM_EXPANSION_DEPTH Maximum allowed expansion depth. None means unlimited. None FIELDS_PARAM Query parameter name for sparse field inclusion. "fields" OMIT_PARAM Query parameter name for explicit exclusions. "omit" RECURSIVE_EXPANSION_PERMITTED Whether recursive expansion patterns are allowed. True WILDCARD_VALUES Wildcard values that mean all fields or all expandable fields. Set to None to disable wildcards. ["*", "~all"]

Example:

REST_FLEX_FIELDS = {
    "EXPAND_PARAM": "include",
}

docs.title: Defining Limits on Serializer Classes
docs.text:
You can set limits directly on a serializer class:

  • maximum_expansion_depth
  • recursive_expansion_permitted

Both settings raise serializers.ValidationError when violated. You can customize the exceptions by overriding recursive_expansion_not_permitted() and expansion_depth_exceeded().

docs.title: Serializer Introspection
docs.text: Instances of FlexFieldsModelSerializer expose expanded_fields, which records the fields expanded during serialization.

docs.title: Wildcards
docs.text:
You can use wildcard values such as * to expand or include all fields at a given level.

Examples:

  • expand=*
  • expand=menu.sections
  • fields=*,restaurant.name,restaurant.address&expand=restaurant

docs.title: Combining Sparse Fields and Expansion
docs.text:
Sparse fieldsets take precedence over expansion. If a field is not explicitly allowed by fields, it will not be expanded even if it appears in expand.

For example, this request omits country from the response because fields wins:

GET /person/13322?fields=id,name&expand=country

Response:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe"
}

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.is_expanded(request, field)
docs.text: Returns whether a field was requested via the active expansion parameter.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.is_included(request, field)
docs.text: Returns whether a field remains included after fields and omit are applied.

docs.title: Query Optimization Backend
docs.text:
An experimental backend can reduce query count and payload size automatically.

REST_FRAMEWORK = {
    "DEFAULT_FILTER_BACKENDS": (
        "rest_flex_fields2.filter_backends.FlexFieldsFilterBackend",
    ),
}

It applies select_related(), prefetch_related(), and only() based on the requested fields and expansions.

Warning

The optimization currently works for one nesting level only. Treat it as a convenience feature, not as a substitute for inspecting your queryset behavior.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2
docs.text:
Public API for the rest_flex_fields2 package.

Exports configuration constants, the main serializer mixin and model serializer, the convenience view set, and the request-inspection helpers is_expanded and is_included.

docs.title: FlexFieldsModelSerializer
docs.text:
Bases: FlexFieldsSerializerMixin, ModelSerializer

Convenience serializer combining FlexFieldsSerializerMixin with ModelSerializer.

Drop-in replacement for serializers.ModelSerializer that adds sparse-fieldset (fields, omit) and nested-expansion (expand) support out of the box.

docs.title: FlexFieldsModelViewSet
docs.text:
Bases: FlexFieldsMixin, ModelViewSet

Convenience view set combining FlexFieldsMixin with ModelViewSet.

Drop-in replacement for viewsets.ModelViewSet with list-action expansion control provided by FlexFieldsMixin.

docs.title: is_expanded
docs.text:
is_expanded(request, field)

Return whether field is requested for expansion.

Inspects the expand query parameter on request. Returns True when field appears in the comma-separated expand list, or when a wildcard value (e.g. * or ~all) is present.

docs.title: is_included
docs.text:
is_included(request, field)

Return whether field should be included in the response.

Returns False when the fields sparse-fieldset parameter is present and field is not listed, or when the omit parameter is present and field is listed. Returns True otherwise.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.serializers.FlexFieldsSerializerMixin
docs.text:
Bases: Serializer

Mixin that adds sparse-fieldset and nested-expansion support to a serializer.

Accepts the fields, omit, and expand keyword arguments (names are configurable via REST_FLEX_FIELDS settings) both as constructor kwargs and as query-string parameters on the current request. Query parameters are only read on the root serializer; nested serializers receive their options through constructor kwargs propagated by the parent.

Declare expandable relations either on Meta.expandable_fields (preferred) or directly on expandable_fields for backwards compatibility.

docs.title: __init__
docs.text:
__init__(*args, **kwargs)

Initialize flex-fields options from kwargs and request query params.

docs.title: get_maximum_expansion_depth
docs.text:
get_maximum_expansion_depth()

Return the effective maximum expansion depth.

Uses the serializer-level maximum_expansion_depth attribute when set, otherwise falls back to the MAXIMUM_EXPANSION_DEPTH setting.

docs.title: get_recursive_expansion_permitted
docs.text:
get_recursive_expansion_permitted()

Return whether recursive expansion is allowed.

Uses the serializer-level recursive_expansion_permitted attribute when set, otherwise falls back to the RECURSIVE_EXPANSION_PERMITTED setting.

docs.title: to_representation
docs.text:
to_representation(instance)

Apply request-sourced flex-fields options once, then delegate to super.

docs.title: get_fields
docs.text:
get_fields()

Return fields after applying constructor-sourced flex-fields options.

docs.title: apply_flex_fields
docs.text:
apply_flex_fields(fields, flex_options)

Apply sparse-fieldset and expansion options to fields in place.

Removes fields that are excluded by omit or not present in fields (sparse-fieldset), then replaces fields listed in expand with their nested serializer instances. Returns the modified fields mapping.

docs.title: recursive_expansion_not_permitted
docs.text:
recursive_expansion_not_permitted()

Raise a validation error indicating recursive expansion.

Override this method to raise a custom exception instead of the default ValidationError.

docs.title: expansion_depth_exceeded
docs.text:
expansion_depth_exceeded()

Raise a validation error indicating the expansion depth limit was exceeded.

Override this method to raise a custom exception instead of the default ValidationError.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.serializers.FlexFieldsModelSerializer
docs.text:
Bases: FlexFieldsSerializerMixin, ModelSerializer

Convenience serializer combining FlexFieldsSerializerMixin with ModelSerializer.

Drop-in replacement for serializers.ModelSerializer that adds sparse-fieldset (fields, omit) and nested-expansion (expand) support out of the box.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.views.FlexFieldsMixin
docs.text:
View mixin that restricts which fields may be expanded on list actions.

Set permit_list_expands to a list of field names that are allowed to be expanded when the view is handling a list request. The allowed names are passed to the serializer via context['permitted_expands'] so that FlexFieldsSerializerMixin can enforce the constraint.

docs.title: get_serializer_context
docs.text:
get_serializer_context()

Extend the serializer context with permitted_expands for list actions.

When the current action is list, adds context['permitted_expands'] populated from permit_list_expands so the serializer can restrict expansion accordingly.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.views.FlexFieldsModelViewSet
docs.text:
Bases: FlexFieldsMixin, ModelViewSet

Convenience view set combining FlexFieldsMixin with ModelViewSet.

Drop-in replacement for viewsets.ModelViewSet with list-action expansion control provided by FlexFieldsMixin.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.filter_backends.FlexFieldsDocsFilterBackend
docs.text:
Bases: BaseFilterBackend

No-op filter backend that adds flex-fields parameters to the API schema.

Does not modify the queryset. Its sole purpose is to expose the fields, omit, and expand query parameters in the OpenAPI schema generated by tools such as drf-spectacular.

docs.title: filter_queryset
docs.text:
filter_queryset(request, queryset, view)

Return queryset unchanged.

docs.title: get_schema_operation_parameters
docs.text:
get_schema_operation_parameters(view)

Return the OpenAPI query parameter definitions for the flex-fields params.

Emits fields, omit, and expand parameter objects for the view's serializer when it is a FlexFieldsSerializerMixin subclass. Returns an empty list for views that do not use flex fields.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.filter_backends.FlexFieldsFilterBackend
docs.text:
Bases: FlexFieldsDocsFilterBackend

Filter backend that optimises querysets based on the active flex-fields options.

Extends FlexFieldsDocsFilterBackend with actual queryset manipulation: applies only() to restrict fetched columns and select_related() / prefetch_related() for expanded relation fields. Only active for GET requests on views whose serializer is a FlexFieldsSerializerMixin subclass.

View-level opt-out attributes:

  • auto_remove_fields_from_query (default True): disable only() optimisation.
  • auto_select_related_on_query (default True): disable relation prefetching.
  • required_query_fields (default []): extra field names always included in the only() call.

docs.title: filter_queryset
docs.text:
filter_queryset(request, queryset, view)

Apply field-selection and relation-prefetch optimisations to queryset.

Resolves the active fields / omit / expand options from the request, then calls only(), select_related(), and prefetch_related() as appropriate. Returns queryset unchanged when the view's serializer is not a FlexFieldsSerializerMixin subclass, or when the request method is not GET.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.utils.is_expanded
docs.text:
is_expanded(request, field)

Return whether field is requested for expansion.

Inspects the expand query parameter on request. Returns True when field appears in the comma-separated expand list, or when a wildcard value (e.g. * or ~all) is present.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.utils.is_included
docs.text:
is_included(request, field)

Return whether field should be included in the response.

Returns False when the fields sparse-fieldset parameter is present and field is not listed, or when the omit parameter is present and field is listed. Returns True otherwise.

docs.title: rest_flex_fields2.utils.split_levels
docs.text:
split_levels(fields)

Split a dot-notation field list into current-level and next-level parts.

Given an iterable such as ['a', 'a.b', 'a.d', 'c'], returns a tuple (first_level, next_level) where first_level is the deduplicated list of top-level names (e.g. ['a', 'c']) and next_level is a dict mapping each name to its remaining path fragments (e.g. {'a': ['b', 'd']}). A plain string is treated as a comma-separated field list.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install the package from PyPI:

pip install drf-flex-fields2

Then subclass FlexFieldsModelSerializer in place of DRF's regular ModelSerializer:

from rest_flex_fields2 import FlexFieldsModelSerializer


class StateSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = State
        fields = ("id", "name")


class CountrySerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Country
        fields = ("id", "name", "population", "states")
        expandable_fields = {
            "states": (StateSerializer, {"many": True}),
        }

If you already have a custom serializer base class, you can instead mix in FlexFieldsSerializerMixin.

docs.title: Optional Django settings
docs.text:
Runtime behavior can be customized with the REST_FLEX_FIELDS setting:

REST_FLEX_FIELDS = {
    "EXPAND_PARAM": "expand",
    "FIELDS_PARAM": "fields",
    "OMIT_PARAM": "omit",
}

The full list of supported settings is documented in Advanced Topics.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
This example shows a serializer that exposes a country relation by primary key unless the client explicitly asks for an expanded nested representation.

from rest_flex_fields2 import FlexFieldsModelSerializer


class StateSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = State
        fields = ("id", "name")


class CountrySerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Country
        fields = ("id", "name", "population", "states")
        expandable_fields = {
            "states": (StateSerializer, {"many": True}),
        }


class PersonSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Person
        fields = ("id", "name", "country", "occupation")
        expandable_fields = {
            "country": CountrySerializer,
        }

Default response:

GET /people/142/

{
    "id": 142,
    "name": "Jim Halpert",
    "country": 1
}

Expanded response:

GET /people/142/?expand=country.states

{
    "id": 142,
    "name": "Jim Halpert",
    "country": {
        "id": 1,
        "name": "United States",
        "states": [
            {
                "id": 23,
                "name": "Ohio"
            },
            {
                "id": 2,
                "name": "Pennsylvania"
            }
        ]
    }
}

From there, continue with Usage for nested expansion, sparse fieldsets, list-view restrictions, and lazy serializer references.

docs.title: Serializer Options
docs.text: Flex-field options can be supplied in three places.

docs.title: 1. Query parameters
docs.text: GET /people?expand=friends.hobbies,employer&omit=age

docs.title: 2. Serializer constructor keyword arguments
docs.text:
serializer = PersonSerializer(
    person,
    expand=["friends.hobbies", "employer"],
    omit=["friends.age"],
)

docs.title: 3. Nested serializer settings in expandable_fields
docs.text:
class PersonSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Person
        fields = ["age", "hobbies", "name"]
        expandable_fields = {
            "friends": (
                "serializer.FriendSerializer",
                {"many": True, "expand": ["hobbies"], "omit": ["age"]},
            ),
        }

docs.title: Supported options
docs.text:
Option Description expand Fields to expand. They must be configured in expandable_fields. fields Fields to include. All other fields are excluded. omit Fields to exclude. All other fields are included.

Query-parameter values accept comma-separated lists. Nested paths use dot notation.

docs.title: Dynamic Field Expansion
docs.text:
To define expandable fields, add an expandable_fields dictionary to the serializer's Meta class. Each key is the field name to expand dynamically. Each value is either a serializer class or a tuple of serializer class plus serializer keyword arguments.

class CountrySerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Country
        fields = ["name", "population"]


class PersonSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    country = serializers.PrimaryKeyRelatedField(read_only=True)

    class Meta:
        model = Person
        fields = ["id", "name", "country", "occupation"]
        expandable_fields = {
            "country": CountrySerializer,
        }

Default response:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "country": 12,
    "occupation": "Programmer"
}

Expanded response for GET /person/13322?expand=country:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "country": {
        "name": "United States",
        "population": 330000000
    },
    "occupation": "Programmer"
}

docs.title: Deferred Fields
docs.text: You can treat a relation as deferred by omitting it from the default field list and defining it only in expandable_fields. The field then appears only when explicitly expanded.

docs.title: Deep Nested Expansion
docs.text:
Nested expansions use dot notation:

class StateSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = State
        fields = ["name", "population"]


class CountrySerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Country
        fields = ["name", "population"]
        expandable_fields = {
            "states": (StateSerializer, {"many": True}),
        }


class PersonSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    country = serializers.PrimaryKeyRelatedField(read_only=True)

    class Meta:
        model = Person
        fields = ["id", "name", "country", "occupation"]
        expandable_fields = {
            "country": CountrySerializer,
        }

Request:

GET /person/13322?expand=country.states

Response:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "occupation": "Programmer",
    "country": {
        "id": 12,
        "name": "United States",
        "states": [
            {
                "name": "Ohio",
                "population": 11000000
            }
        ]
    }
}

Be deliberate with nested expansions on large result sets. They can increase query count substantially unless you pair them with select_related() or prefetch_related().

docs.title: Expansion on List Views
docs.text:
Subclass FlexFieldsModelViewSet when you want to limit which fields may be expanded on list endpoints.

from rest_flex_fields2 import FlexFieldsModelViewSet, is_expanded


class PersonViewSet(FlexFieldsModelViewSet):
    permit_list_expands = ["employer"]
    serializer_class = PersonSerializer

    def get_queryset(self):
        queryset = models.Person.objects.all()

        if is_expanded(self.request, "employer"):
            queryset = queryset.select_related("employer")

        return queryset

docs.title: Expanding a To-Many Relationship
docs.text:
Set many=True in the serializer options when the expanded relation returns multiple objects.

class CountrySerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Country
        fields = ["name", "population"]
        expandable_fields = {
            "states": (StateSerializer, {"many": True}),
        }

docs.title: Sparse Fieldsets
docs.text:
Use either fields or omit to control the output shape.

Default response:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "country": {
        "name": "United States",
        "population": 330000000
    },
    "occupation": "Programmer",
    "hobbies": ["rock climbing", "sipping coffee"]
}

Request with ?fields=id,name,country:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "country": {
        "name": "United States",
        "population": 330000000
    }
}

Request with ?fields=id,name,country.name:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "country": {
        "name": "United States"
    }
}

Request with ?omit=country:

{
    "id": 13322,
    "name": "John Doe",
    "occupation": "Programmer",
    "hobbies": ["rock climbing", "sipping coffee"]
}

docs.title: Lazy Serializer References
docs.text:
To avoid circular imports, reference a serializer lazily by dotted path:

expandable_fields = {
    "record_set": ("<module_path_to_serializer_class>.RelatedSerializer", {"many": True}),
}

Fully qualified import paths are supported. Legacy <app>.serializers.SerializerName paths still work as well.

docs.title: Serializer Reuse
docs.text:
fields and omit can also be passed directly to nested serializer instances, which helps avoid maintaining multiple slightly different serializers.

from rest_flex_fields2 import FlexFieldsModelSerializer


class CountrySerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Country
        fields = ["id", "name", "population", "capital", "square_miles"]


class PersonSerializer(FlexFieldsModelSerializer):
    country = CountrySerializer(fields=["id", "name"])

    class Meta:
        model = Person
        fields = ["id", "name", "country"]
